channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - techraptor.net
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for techraptor.net

item.title: AION 2's Western Release Promises Epic Flying Battles, Hundreds Of Dungeons, And Low Ping For Global Fans
item.content:encoded:
AION 2, the popular Korean MMO from NCSoft, announced this week that its western launch will take place in September 2026. With this, the sequel to the original Aeon, which launched back in 2008, will finally be available to players all over the world.

At Summer Game Fest, I had a chance to sit down and talk with Daniel LaFuente, Director of Operations, Merv Lee Kwai, Executive Publishing Producer for AION 2, and Seung-Uk Baek, the Chief Business Officer for NCSoft, to see some of what the game has to offer and to discuss the western release.

AION 2 is a direct continuation of the events of the first AION. With the release of the original game, it was revealed that there was a lot more that was wanted to be done with AION from when it was originally released, but “the technology wasn’t ready for any of that.”

Now that we’re 13 years later, for NCSoft, that technology is ready for them to create the MMORPG that they always wanted to release. Some big changes to AION 2 don’t just include massive graphical overhauls, but also a “living and breathing world that’s seamless to move through that you can run, fly, and swim through.”

To show us what the game has to offer, we were walked through the character creation process, we were shown a variety of locations across the continent, and we got to see a whole host of mounts and wings, the wings being a core aspect of AION 2’s gameplay. 

Moving quickly through the character customizer, we were treated to views of a large number of preset creations. Customization not only for hair styles and body styles, but you can get really granular with what you want to change. You can get as focused as raising and lowering cheekbones.

Scrolling quickly through the different outfit customization options, we were quickly shown 800+ themed outfit pieces that could be mixed and matched to create unique and stunning outfits.

The Wings in AION are a key part of the experience; every character gets their own set of wings for traversal and in some places, combat. In the original AION, wings were used to glide, and there was some free-flight allowed in certain cities, but it certainly wasn’t something you could do everywhere. Here in AION 2, you can use your wings to fly freely at any time.

Launch Content For AION 2

At launch, AION will have 200 dungeons split across four different types. Sealed Dungeons are the entry-level combat experiences; get in and clear out the enemies before re-emerging with your hard-won loot.

Nightmare is the first instanced dungeons. These are specifically solo-play dungeons, pitting you against monsters and a boss on your own.

For 4 players, you’ll be able to participate in the Expedition dungeons, which are the entry level for cooperative play. For those who want even more, the 8-player raids are called Sanctuary.

For all of these dungeons, every player will earn rewards of some kind. It was important for the design methodology of the game that no matter what, all of the players are being rewarded.

AION 2 falls into a weird space where they’re the sequel to an incredibly popular MMO, but the first AION is still currently available to play. Asking Baek what the incentives might be for those players who have sunk hundreds of hours into AION to move over and essentially start over in AION 2 I was informed that while there isn’t a specific bonus or reward for ‘returning’ players that they were more than sure that all of the gameplay, combat, and graphical upgrades would be more than enough to get players excited to dive into AION 2.

By a somewhat similar sentiment, I was interested to know for those players who were already experiencing AION 2 through VPNs, why they would want to load up a new character on the global version of the game. Similarly, the benefit here is in the gameplay improvement that players will get from not having to play on a high-ping server. Based on the big support that AION 2 plans to have for players across the US, they expect even in remote locations, players to drop to less than 40 ping.

Andrew has written Video Game and Entertainment news, reviews, and guides for 10+ years. As Senior Content Manager, he assists in creating and editing… More about Andrew



https://techraptor.net/gaming/features/aion-2s-western-release-promises-epic-flying-battles-hundreds-of-dungeons-and-low
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Slap Out of It! Throws Cartoon Logic And Gags Your Way In A Seriously Good Puzzle Game - Interview
item.content:encoded:
A genre that I love and will always happily play more of is puzzle games. Games that stretch my brain for the solution, and stretch the idea of a puzzle in a way that only video games could. I got the chance to get my hands on Slap Out of It!, a new 'slap-and-solve' puzzle game from Turbo Button, where your new job at Puzzlr has you solving puzzles in the weirdest way.

While I was at Summer Game Fest, I didn't just get to experience the early stages of Slap Out of It!, but I also got to talk with Holden Link, Co-Founder at Turbo Button, responsible for programming and sound design for the game.

Slap Out of It! takes place over the course of your first day at puzzle-solving company, Puzzlr. You play as an orange-skinned muppet-like creature in a world of similarly weird denizens with even weirder problems.

When exploring the opening sequence of Slap Out of It!, it felt like two out of every three characters belonged in the Muppets, while that remaining third had broken loose from a Squanch Games title like Accounting or Trover Saves the Universe.

The tools of the trade in Slap Out of It! are, as you might expect, your slapping hands. Look at what you want to interact with, and press one of the trigger buttons to shoot out your hand to interact, pick up, put down, or adjust an item. This simple way to interact with the world is also assisted by your slaps being kindly guided towards any key item or interactable element that you might be looking at nearby.

Slap Out of It! also felt very reminiscent of early point-and-click adventures in that there's all manner of things to slap and get a reaction from, even if they aren't in any way important to progression.

Asking about the origins of the idea for Slap Out of It! Link told me, "We were trying to make a homage to some of our favorite point and click games, but do something more animation heavy and slapstick. We absolutely love a lot of 3D puzzle games out there, but one thing we've noticed compared to a lot of older games is that they don't have quite as many interactive characters in the environments."

A lot of [3D puzzle games] are you exploring alone

"We wanted to throw back to some of our favorite ones from the 90s and 2000s and bring characters back into them to be really interactive."

Link further explained that at the genesis of the game, slapping "was not always the primary mechanic, but if very much became that way over time."

In the break room of Puzzlr, where you'll be for the early tutorial of the game, there are stacked cups, overfilled trash cans, and even a punching bag with a scary face on it with a pro-union speech bubble to beat up.

During the tutorial, it's your job to solve the problems of a few customers who enter the lobby of Puzzlr. This includes a man whose head is on fire, where the solution is to (very satisfyingly) 'break glass' to obtain a fire extinguisher to help him out.

Talking about the final product, Link said that the team at Turbo Button is aiming to have the final game time be around 6-10 hours, but remarked, "It varies a lot with puzzle gamers because it can take people different amounts of time to solve puzzles, and some people are also going to be trying to hunt for all the collectibles."

The collectibles that players will be able to find in-game are cosmetic alterations for your character's arms. This was necessary because you don't ever actually see the player character when in-game.

I really enjoyed how much the puzzles immediately leaned hard into cartoon logic. When a man approached with $1 to purchase a $2 puzzle, I simply needed to slap open the photocopier, slap the dollar bill onto the copier, and then slap the copy button to get a second dollar. At this point, the printer jammed, and by jammed, I mean it began to ooze grape jam that needed to be used in a later puzzle.

The logic behind the puzzles continued to lean into the ludicrous as I came across a laser-protected dinosaur bone, a rave for birds where entry requires you to wear green sunglasses, and an entire bathroom that can rotate upside down, where you need to strategically place the window in order to steal an item from the room above, and drop an item into the room below.

Asking about how it was different to approach puzzle design for a game where everything obeys the laws of physics to one with cartoon logic, Link explained, "I think for us the biggest thing with our tutorial is not really teaching you the controls, it's trying to get you thinking with cartoon logic. The more we can key players behaving in a silly way, the more likely they are to try silly things to solve these puzzles."

There's certainly a lot of real thinking involved, but if you're just exploring stuff and slapping things that's gonna usually give you the information you're looking for.

Slap Out of It! is releasing next year and can be wishlisted on Steam now. Link did say that they are planning on releasing on console as well as PC, but they're a four-person indie team, so they're trying not to over-commit, so what specific consoles it will be coming to he wasn't able to say.

Andrew has written Video Game and Entertainment news, reviews, and guides for 10+ years. As Senior Content Manager, he assists in creating and editing… More about Andrew



https://techraptor.net/gaming/previews/slap-out-of-it-throws-cartoon-logic-and-gags-your-way-in-seriously-good-puzzle-game
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Moss: The Forgotten Relic is a Much-Needed VR-Free Re-Release
item.content:encoded:
While VR has always been a great technology for enthusiasts, it does present something of a barrier to entry that can be insurmountable for some. That’s why it’s always good to see previously VR-only titles getting a VR-free release, and why I was so happy to be offered a chance for this Moss: The Forgotten Relic preview. It’s finally a chance to enjoy the mouse-centric 3D platformer without a television strapped directly to my head.

If you’re not lucky enough to own a VR device yourself, or you’ve just missed the original release, the Moss is a series of 3D platformers where you help a small adventurous mouse called Quill through a series of fantasy-themed adventures. Along the way, you personally take part in the story as ‘The Reader’, a human-sized entity who can interact with various elements in the world to help out your tiny companion. Moss: The Forgotten Relic is a non-VR re-release of both Moss and Moss: Book II that brings the series to the Switch and Xbox platforms for the first time, to boot.

The elements of the games are still there, with the only difference being the lack of any VR or motion-controller support. You’ve still got to interact with the world to make it more passible for Quill, but now all of those controls are simply mapped to the right-side of the controller. I was given the opportunity to sit down with the game’s demo before its release later this year to get a vertical slice of the Moss: Book II half of the game.

What’s Different

While I may not have played the specific part of the game that I was demoing before, I do have a decent amount of experience with the original Moss thanks to PSVR, so I was reasonably sure I knew what to expect from this release. Moss: The Forgotten Relic provides you with all of the jumping, combat, and puzzles of the original games, but without the ability to control your view by moving your head around.

From the outset, the vibe is very subdued, both stylistically and mechanically. You have the ability to heal Quill whenever you want, so there’s not much danger outside of getting swarmed in combat, at least, during normal gameplay. You’re mostly free to enjoy the environments and take your time trying to solve the puzzles, which is great because the inability to move your head around the environments does mean you’ll need to spend more time hunting for collectibles if you’re that way inclined.

Adorable & Explorable

Even with such a small slice of the game, it’s easy to see why it’s great that Moss is getting a non-VR release at last. Quill is possibly one of the cutest characters in gaming, and the unique world-interaction mechanics mean that there’s always something interesting going on. It’s also good that the preview showed off the Book II half of Moss: The Forgotten Relic, because the combat has also been expanded over what was on offer in the first game, even including a thrilling-looking boss fight right at the end of my preview time.

While the base combat can get a bit same-y after a while, the great variety of puzzles and the updates to combat do promise a deeper 3D platformer experience. The biggest concern is simply whether or not the collectibles are going to be semi-impossible to find without being able to visually scan the environment outside of VR. Though there’s every chance that the developers will think of a way around it before the final release, and to look at it another way, it means you really have to dig into the lush environments a bit more. 

Final Thoughts

Though my time with it was all too brief, Moss: The Forgotten Relic is a much-awaited re-release that seems like it’s going to finally provide everyone with a way of enjoying this adorable animal adventure, VR-owner or not. While some of the ‘wow’ factor might be missing along with the VR support, the gameplay is still as worthwhile and enjoyable an experience as it was before. Just keep an extra eye out for those collectibles in case you end up missing a bunch of them behind walls or corners.

Moss: The Forgotten Relic was previewed on PC via Steam with a copy provided by the publisher over the course of 30 Minutes - All images were taken during the course of this Preview.

Will has been writing about video games professionally since 2016 and has covered everything from AAA game reviews to industry events and everything in… More about William



https://techraptor.net/gaming/previews/moss-forgotten-relic
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
